params.text:
Hazards in care settings

A hazard is anything with the potential to cause harm; the risk is how likely it is to happen and how serious it could be. Learning to spot hazards is a skill you use on every shift.

  • Slips, trips and falls — wet floors, trailing cables, poor lighting, rugs and clutter.
  • Manual handling — moving people or heavy loads without the right technique or equipment.
  • Biological — infection from bodily fluids, contaminated waste or poor hand hygiene.
  • Chemical — cleaning products, disinfectants and medicines (covered by COSHH).
  • Physical environment — hot water and radiators (scalds and burns), faulty equipment, fire.
  • Violence and aggression — including distressed behaviour linked to dementia or pain.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Match each common care hazard to a sensible control. Tap to reveal.

dialogs.text: Water on a bathroom or sluice-room floor
dialogs.answer: Clean up spills straight away, use wet-floor signs, fit slip-resistant flooring and keep a cleaning schedule.

dialogs.text: Very hot water at a tap or bath
dialogs.answer: Thermostatic mixing valves limit outlet temperature; always check bath water before use to prevent scalds.
Keep walkways clear, tape or route cables safely, and make sure lighting is adequate.

dialogs.text: Cleaning chemicals stored on an open shelf
dialogs.answer: Store securely following COSHH guidance, keep them out of reach of vulnerable people, and follow the product safety data sheet.

dialogs.text: A resident becoming distressed and aggressive during care
dialogs.answer: Stay calm, give space, use known de-escalation from their care plan, and record and report the incident.
